persons.arguments_against: The strongest case against: 12 officials share the surname Nevill in the staff lists, so a same-name relative could conceivably hold a Newfoundland post; and the bio appears in only one edition (1886), so the link rests on one printed entry. This fails because the office is a unique compound title — 'Inspector of lighthouses and superintendent of public buildings' — which matches the staff-list position verbatim, in the same colony, with both initials ('J. T.') agreeing exactly, the bio's appointment date (Jan. 1874) preceding and consistent with the stint's first listing (1877), and a same-edition (1886) co-occurrence at position similarity 100. A colony has exactly one Inspector of Lighthouses and Superintendent of Public Buildings at a time; no second J. T. Nevill exists to contest it.

evidence_cited.fact: Inspector of lighthouses and superintendent of public buildings, Newfoundland, Jan., 1874
J. T. Nevill | Inspector of Lighthouses and Superintendent of Public Buildings

persons.would_overturn: Discovery of a second J. T. Nevill in Newfoundland service (e.g. a son succeeding the father in the same office between 1877 and 1897), or evidence the biography's subject died or left the colony before 1877.

notes: Exact two-initial agreement plus a verbatim match on a unique compound senior office in the same colony, with 1886 edition co-occurrence; this is as strong as a no-honours case gets.
